devanagari: मैत्रेय उवाच । चरमः सद्विशेषाणां अनेकोऽसंयुतः सदा । परमाणुः स विज्ञेयो नृणामैक्यभ्रमो यतः ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: maitreya uvācacaramaḥ sadviśeṣāṇāṃ aneko'saṃyutaḥ sadāparamāṇuḥ sa vijñeyo nṛṇāmaikyabhramo yataḥ
translation:
Maitreya said:
The ultimate irreducible particle of the parts of a gross effect (substance), which is ever separate (i.e. has not reached the stage of being an effect), and which is not combined with another in an aggregate, is called an atom (para- māṇu)[1]. It is from the aggregation of atoms that men get the illusion of whole substance.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
It may be noted that the Bhāgavata Purāṇa and the Veḍānta (vide Brahma Sūtra II.2.12-17) do not subscribe to the atomic theory of the Vaiśeṣikas.

devanagari: सत एव पदार्थस्य स्वरूपावस्थितस्य यत् । कैवल्यं परममहान् अविशेषो निरन्तरः ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: sata eva padārthasya svarūpāvasthitasya yatkaivalyaṃ paramamahān aviśeṣo nirantaraḥ
translation: When the idea of peculiarity or of differences is separated (not considered), the entire substance in its essence which remains unaltered (with no change of dimensions) is called parama mahān (maximum dimension)[2].
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Having the duration of two parārdhas—Kramasandarbha.

devanagari: एवं कालोऽप्यनुमितः सौक्ष्म्ये स्थौल्ये च सत्तम । संस्थानभुक्त्या भगवान् अव्यक्तो व्यक्तभुग्विभुः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ kālo'pyanumitaḥ saukṣmye sthaulye ca sattamasaṃsthānabhuktyā bhagavān avyakto vyaktabhugvibhuḥ
translation: Oh Vidura, Time is also inferred as subtle, (medium), and the longest according as it pervades the atomic, medium, and the grossest (parama mahān) matter. It is God’s power which itself remains unmanifest, but occupies and encompasses the manifested substance and is competent to manage creation etc. of the universe.

devanagari: स कालः परमाणुर्वै यो भुङ्क्ते परमाणुताम् । सतोऽविशेषभुग्यस्तु स कालः परमो महान् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sa kālaḥ paramāṇurvai yo bhuṅkte paramāṇutāmsato'viśeṣabhugyastu sa kālaḥ paramo mahān
translation: That much period of time which is required to occupy an atom is the atomic period. That which is required to enjoy the matter in its entirety, is called parama mahān (the largest general—required to cross the totality of twelve Rāśis or Bhuvanakośa i.e. a period of a year and a period up to two parārdhas by the recurrence of the years.

devanagari: अणुर्द्वौ परमाणू स्यात् त्रसरेणुस्त्रयः स्मृतः । जालार्करश्म्यवगतः खमेवानुपतन्नगात् ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: aṇurdvau paramāṇū syāt trasareṇustrayaḥ smṛtaḥjālārkaraśmyavagataḥ khamevānupatannagāt
translation: [3] Two paramāṇus make one aṇu. Three aṇus make one trasareṇu. It is the smallest particle which is visible and is noticed to be going up in the ray of the Sun coming through a window. The time which is required io occupy by three trasareṇus is called a truṭi. Hundred truṭis make up one vedha. Three vedhas is regarded as a lava. A nimiṣa is known to consist of three lavas. Three nimiṣas make up a kṣaṇa. Five kṣaṇas are said to form one kāṣṭhā. Fifteen kāṣṭhās make one laghu. Fifteen laghus are said to constitute one nāḍikā (or ghaṭikā). Two nāḍikās make one muhūrta. Six or seven nāḍikās make one prahara of men. It is also called yāma.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
These verses contain the following measures of time:—
2 paramāṇus = 1 aṇu; 3 aṇus= 1 trasareṇu; 3 trasareṇus = 1 truṭi; 100 truṭis = 1 vedha; 3 vedhas = 1 lava; 3 lavas = 1 nimiṣa; 3 nimiṣas = 1 kṣaṇa; 5 kṣaṇas = 1 kāṣṭhā; 15 kāṣṭhās = 1 laghu; 15 laghus = nāḍikā; 2 nāḍikās = 1 muhūrta; 6/7 nāḍikās = 1 yāma or prahara.

devanagari: त्रसरेणुत्रिकं भुङ्क्ते यः कालः स त्रुटिः स्मृतः । शतभागस्तु वेधः स्यात् तैस्त्रिभिस्तु लवः स्मृतः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: trasareṇutrikaṃ bhuṅkte yaḥ kālaḥ sa truṭiḥ smṛtaḥśatabhāgastu vedhaḥ syāt taistribhistu lavaḥ smṛtaḥ
translation: [3] Two paramāṇus make one aṇu. Three aṇus make one trasareṇu. It is the smallest particle which is visible and is noticed to be going up in the ray of the Sun coming through a window. The time which is required io occupy by three trasareṇus is called a truṭi. Hundred truṭis make up one vedha. Three vedhas is regarded as a lava. A nimiṣa is known to consist of three lavas. Three nimiṣas make up a kṣaṇa. Five kṣaṇas are said to form one kāṣṭhā. Fifteen kāṣṭhās make one laghu. Fifteen laghus are said to constitute one nāḍikā (or ghaṭikā). Two nāḍikās make one muhūrta. Six or seven nāḍikās make one prahara of men. It is also called yāma.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
These verses contain the following measures of time:—
2 paramāṇus = 1 aṇu; 3 aṇus= 1 trasareṇu; 3 trasareṇus = 1 truṭi; 100 truṭis = 1 vedha; 3 vedhas = 1 lava; 3 lavas = 1 nimiṣa; 3 nimiṣas = 1 kṣaṇa; 5 kṣaṇas = 1 kāṣṭhā; 15 kāṣṭhās = 1 laghu; 15 laghus = nāḍikā; 2 nāḍikās = 1 muhūrta; 6/7 nāḍikās = 1 yāma or prahara.

devanagari: निमेषस्त्रिलवो ज्ञेय आम्नातस्ते त्रयः क्षणः । क्षणान् पञ्च विदुः काष्ठां लघु ता दश पञ्च च ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: nimeṣastrilavo jñeya āmnātaste trayaḥ kṣaṇaḥkṣaṇān pañca viduḥ kāṣṭhāṃ laghu tā daśa pañca ca
translation: [3] Two paramāṇus make one aṇu. Three aṇus make one trasareṇu. It is the smallest particle which is visible and is noticed to be going up in the ray of the Sun coming through a window. The time which is required io occupy by three trasareṇus is called a truṭi. Hundred truṭis make up one vedha. Three vedhas is regarded as a lava. A nimiṣa is known to consist of three lavas. Three nimiṣas make up a kṣaṇa. Five kṣaṇas are said to form one kāṣṭhā. Fifteen kāṣṭhās make one laghu. Fifteen laghus are said to constitute one nāḍikā (or ghaṭikā). Two nāḍikās make one muhūrta. Six or seven nāḍikās make one prahara of men. It is also called yāma.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
These verses contain the following measures of time:—
2 paramāṇus = 1 aṇu; 3 aṇus= 1 trasareṇu; 3 trasareṇus = 1 truṭi; 100 truṭis = 1 vedha; 3 vedhas = 1 lava; 3 lavas = 1 nimiṣa; 3 nimiṣas = 1 kṣaṇa; 5 kṣaṇas = 1 kāṣṭhā; 15 kāṣṭhās = 1 laghu; 15 laghus = nāḍikā; 2 nāḍikās = 1 muhūrta; 6/7 nāḍikās = 1 yāma or prahara.

devanagari: लघूनि वै समाम्नाता दश पञ्च च नाडिका । ते द्वे मुहूर्तः प्रहरः षड्यामः सप्त वा नृणाम् ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: laghūni vai samāmnātā daśa pañca ca nāḍikāte dve muhūrtaḥ praharaḥ ṣaḍyāmaḥ sapta vā nṛṇām
translation: [3] Two paramāṇus make one aṇu. Three aṇus make one trasareṇu. It is the smallest particle which is visible and is noticed to be going up in the ray of the Sun coming through a window. The time which is required io occupy by three trasareṇus is called a truṭi. Hundred truṭis make up one vedha. Three vedhas is regarded as a lava. A nimiṣa is known to consist of three lavas. Three nimiṣas make up a kṣaṇa. Five kṣaṇas are said to form one kāṣṭhā. Fifteen kāṣṭhās make one laghu. Fifteen laghus are said to constitute one nāḍikā (or ghaṭikā). Two nāḍikās make one muhūrta. Six or seven nāḍikās make one prahara of men. It is also called yāma.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
These verses contain the following measures of time:—
2 paramāṇus = 1 aṇu; 3 aṇus= 1 trasareṇu; 3 trasareṇus = 1 truṭi; 100 truṭis = 1 vedha; 3 vedhas = 1 lava; 3 lavas = 1 nimiṣa; 3 nimiṣas = 1 kṣaṇa; 5 kṣaṇas = 1 kāṣṭhā; 15 kāṣṭhās = 1 laghu; 15 laghus = nāḍikā; 2 nāḍikās = 1 muhūrta; 6/7 nāḍikās = 1 yāma or prahara.

devanagari: द्वादशार्धपलोन्मानं चतुर्भिश्चतुरङ्गुलैः । स्वर्णमाषैः कृतच्छिद्रं यावत् प्रस्थजलप्लुतम् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: dvādaśārdhapalonmānaṃ caturbhiścaturaṅgulaiḥsvarṇamāṣaiḥ kṛtacchidraṃ yāvat prasthajalaplutam
translation: A copper vessel of six palas in weight and with sides sixteen aṅgulas in height, and with a capacity to contain a prastha of water, and with a hole (of the dimension) of a gold wire four aṅgulas in length and four māṣas (= I /3rd Tola) in weight—the time required to submerge (in water) such a vessel is called nāḍikā (ghaṭikā).

devanagari: यामाश्चत्वारश्चत्वारो मर्त्यानामहनी उभे । पक्षः पञ्चदशाहानि शुक्लः कृष्णश्च मानद ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: yāmāścatvāraścatvāro martyānāmahanī ubhepakṣaḥ pañcadaśāhāni śuklaḥ kṛṣṇaśca mānada
translation: Oh respectful Vidura! Four and four yāmas make a day and a night of human beings. Fifteen days make one pakṣa. It is bright and dark.

devanagari: तयोः समुच्चयो मासः पितॄणां तदहर्निशम् । द्वौ तावृतुः षडयनं दक्षिणं चोत्तरं दिवि ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: tayoḥ samuccayo māsaḥ pitṝṇāṃ tadaharniśamdvau tāvṛtuḥ ṣaḍayanaṃ dakṣiṇaṃ cottaraṃ divi
translation: The aggregate of two pakṣas makes one month which is a day and a night of the pitṛs (departed forefathers). Two months make a ṛtu (season). Six months make one ayana. It is northern (when the Sun apparently moves to the north) and the southern (when the Sun appears to move to the south of the equator).

devanagari: अयने चाहनी प्राहुः वत्सरो द्वादश स्मृतः । संवत्सरशतं नॄणां परमायुर्निरूपितम् ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: ayane cāhanī prāhuḥ vatsaro dvādaśa smṛtaḥsaṃvatsaraśataṃ nṝṇāṃ paramāyurnirūpitam
translation: The ayanas are called the day and the night of the Svarga. A year is made up of twelve months. One hundred years is said to be the maximum life of human beings.[4]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā adds that the maximum life of Pitṛs and gods is one hundred years according to their respective measures of time.

devanagari: ग्रहर्क्षताराचक्रस्थः परमाण्वादिना जगत् । संवत्सरावसानेन पर्येत्यनिमिषो विभुः ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: graharkṣatārācakrasthaḥ paramāṇvādinā jagatsaṃvatsarāvasānena paryetyanimiṣo vibhuḥ
translation: The ever-vigilant controlling god (the Sun) occupies the sphere consisting of the planets, the nakṣatras (e.g. Aśvinī, Bharaṇī and other constellations) and other stars and revolves round the world beginning with atomic division of time and ending with one year (the period required to cross the bhuvana-kośa).

devanagari: संवत्सरः परिवत्सर इडावत्सर एव च । अनुवत्सरो वत्सरश्च विदुरैवं प्रभाष्यते ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: saṃvatsaraḥ parivatsara iḍāvatsara eva caanuvatsaro vatsaraśca viduraivaṃ prabhāṣyate
translation: Oh Vidura, the period of a year is thus called saṃvatsara (the Solar year), parivatsara (related to Jupiter) Iḍāvatsara (of 360 days—savana) anuvatsara (the lunar year) and vatsara (related to nakṣatras).

devanagari: यः सृज्यशक्तिमुरुधोच्छ्वसयन् स्वशक्त्या । पुंसोऽभ्रमाय दिवि धावति भूतभेदः । कालाख्यया गुणमयं क्रतुभिर्वितन्वन् । तस्मै बलिं हरत वत्सरपञ...
english_devnagari: yaḥ sṛjyaśaktimurudhocchvasayan svaśaktyāpuṃso'bhramāya divi dhāvati bhūtabhedaḥkālākhyayā guṇamayaṃ kratubhirvitanvantasmai baliṃ harata vatsarapañcakāya
translation: [5] Offer your worship to the God Sun who made the five kinds of the year and who by his own power (in the form of kāla) urges the powers of things to develop into effects (gross forms) in different ways, and who is a part of the element called tejas. He runs through the sky for removing the delusion of men (as if by dissuading them from waste of life in pleasures) and extending the fruits of guṇas by performance of sacrifices.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Padaratnāvalī takes this verse as applicable to god Viṣṇu: Perform your sacrifices to Hari the antaryāmin, of the five kinds of the year, etc.

devanagari: विदुर उवाच । पितृदेवमनुष्याणां आयुः परमिदं स्मृतम् । परेषां गतिमाचक्ष्व ये स्युः कल्पाद्‍बहिर्विदः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: vidura uvācapitṛdevamanuṣyāṇāṃ āyuḥ paramidaṃ smṛtampareṣāṃ gatimācakṣva ye syuḥ kalpād‍bahirvidaḥ
translation:
Vidura said:
The maximum span of life of the pitṛs (manes), gods and men has been stated, (as hundred years according to the measure of time of these). Please tell me the state (life-span) of those learned ones who stay beyond the three worlds.

devanagari: भगवान् वेद कालस्य गतिं भगवतो ननु । विश्वं विचक्षते धीरा योगराद्धेन चक्षुषा ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavān veda kālasya gatiṃ bhagavato nanuviśvaṃ vicakṣate dhīrā yogarāddhena cakṣuṣā
translation: Verily, you the revered one know the course of Time which is capable of destroying everything. The wise ones can visualize the universe by their yogic vision.

devanagari: मैत्रेय उवाच । कृतं त्रेता द्वापरं च कलिश्चेति चतुर्युगम् । दिव्यैर्द्वादशभिर्वर्षैः सावधानं निरूपितम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: maitreya uvācakṛtaṃ tretā dvāparaṃ ca kaliśceti caturyugamdivyairdvādaśabhirvarṣaiḥ sāvadhānaṃ nirūpitam
translation:
Maitreya said:
It is said that the cycle of four yugas, viz. kṛta, tretā, dvāpara and kali along with their sandhis (transitional periods at the beginning of a yuga) and aṃśas (the transitional period at the end of ayuga), consist of twelve thousand years of gods.

devanagari: चत्वारि त्रीणि द्वे चैकं कृतादिषु यथाक्रमम् । सङ्ख्यातानि सहस्राणि द्विगुणानि शतानि च ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: catvāri trīṇi dve caikaṃ kṛtādiṣu yathākramamsaṅkhyātāni sahasrāṇi dviguṇāni śatāni ca
translation: [6] The period of yugas respectively (of kṛta etc.) is four thousand, three thousand, two thousand and one thousand (divine) years. The sandhyā period at the beginning and the aṃśa period at the end (of each yuga) is respectively eight hundred, six hundred, four hundred and two hundred years (of gods). The learned ones designate the period between sandhyā and aṃśa as yuga, and the (special) laws of conduct (dharma) have been ordained with reference to the (particular) yuga.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
The classification of the Yugas terms of divine (gods’) years is as follows:

devanagari: संध्यांशयोरन्तरेण यः कालः शतसङ्ख्ययोः । तमेवाहुर्युगं तज्ज्ञा यत्र धर्मो विधीयते ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: saṃdhyāṃśayorantareṇa yaḥ kālaḥ śatasaṅkhyayoḥtamevāhuryugaṃ tajjñā yatra dharmo vidhīyate
translation: [6] The period of yugas respectively (of kṛta etc.) is four thousand, three thousand, two thousand and one thousand (divine) years. The sandhyā period at the beginning and the aṃśa period at the end (of each yuga) is respectively eight hundred, six hundred, four hundred and two hundred years (of gods). The learned ones designate the period between sandhyā and aṃśa as yuga, and the (special) laws of conduct (dharma) have been ordained with reference to the (particular) yuga.
purport:
Footnote [6]:
The classification of the Yugas terms of divine (gods’) years is as follows:

devanagari: धर्मश्चतुष्पान्मनुजान् कृते समनुवर्तते । स एवान्येष्वधर्मेण व्येति पादेन वर्धता ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: dharmaścatuṣpānmanujān kṛte samanuvartatesa evānyeṣvadharmeṇa vyeti pādena vardhatā
translation: In the kṛta age, dharma accompanied men on (all his) four legs (viz. penance, purity, compassion and truth). With the increase of irreligion (adharma), religion became diminished by one leg, in each of the other yugas.

devanagari: त्रिलोक्या युगसाहस्रं बहिराब्रह्मणो दिनम् । तावत्येव निशा तात यन्निमीलति विश्वसृक् ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: trilokyā yugasāhasraṃ bahirābrahmaṇo dinamtāvatyeva niśā tāta yannimīlati viśvasṛk
translation: Oh Vidura, beyond the three worlds (from mahar loka) to Brahma Ioka, a day consists of one thousand cycles of four yugas. The night is also of the same duration when the Creator of the universe goes to sleep.

devanagari: निशावसान आरब्धो लोककल्पोऽनुवर्तते । यावद्दिनं भगवतो मनून् भुञ्जंश्चतुर्दश ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: niśāvasāna ārabdho lokakalpo'nuvartateyāvaddinaṃ bhagavato manūn bhuñjaṃścaturdaśa
translation: When it is the nightfall without any moon or the sun (in existence), the three worlds bhūḥ, bhuvaḥ, suvaḥ lie concealed in him.

devanagari: स्वं स्वं कालं मनुर्भुङ्क्ते साधिकां ह्येकसप्ततिम् । मन्वन्तरेषु मनवः तद् वंश्या ऋषयः सुराः । भवन्ति चैव युगपत् सुरेशाश्चानु ये च तान् ...
english_devnagari: svaṃ svaṃ kālaṃ manurbhuṅkte sādhikāṃ hyekasaptatimmanvantareṣu manavaḥ tad vaṃśyā ṛṣayaḥ surāḥbhavanti caiva yugapat sureśāścānu ye ca tān
translation: Every Manu rules during his own period which is somewhat longer than seventy-one catur-yugas (cycles of four yugas[7]) In the eras of Manus, kings in the lineage of the Manu are born in succession. Hermits, gods, king of gods and his attendants are born simultaneously.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
This line indicating the vagueness implied in ‘longer than’ (sādhikā) is repeated in other Purāṇas also (e.g. Viṣṇu P. 1.3.18). Baldev Upadhyaya on the strength of Vāyu Purāṇa. (57.35) suggests that it should be emended as ‘sandhikā’ (viḍe Purāṇa Vimarśa,pp. 291-93). The Bhāgavata Purāṇa seems to have adopted the reading from the Viṣṇu Purāṇa. which is generally accepted as earlier than the Bhāgavata Purāṇa. vide Purāṇa Vimarśa. pp. 542-45.

devanagari: एष दैनन्दिनः सर्गो ब्राह्मस्त्रैलोक्यवर्तनः । तिर्यङ्नृपितृदेवानां सम्भवो यत्र कर्मभिः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: eṣa dainandinaḥ sargo brāhmastrailokyavartanaḥtiryaṅnṛpitṛdevānāṃ sambhavo yatra karmabhiḥ
translation: This is Brahma’s daily creation whereby the three worlds are made to function and in which the birds, beasts, men, pitṛs and gods are born according to their karmas.

devanagari: मन्वन्तरेषु भगवान् बिभ्रत्सत्त्वं स्वमूर्तिभिः । मन्वादिभिरिदं विश्वं अवत्युदितपौरुषः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: manvantareṣu bhagavān bibhratsattvaṃ svamūrtibhiḥmanvādibhiridaṃ viśvaṃ avatyuditapauruṣaḥ
translation: In the Manu-eras, the Supreme Lord retains his sattvaguṇa and protects the universe by incarnating as Manus and manifesting himself in other human forms.

devanagari: तमोमात्रामुपादाय प्रतिसंरुद्धविक्रमः । कालेनानुगताशेष आस्ते तूष्णीं दिनात्यये ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: tamomātrāmupādāya pratisaṃruddhavikramaḥkālenānugatāśeṣa āste tūṣṇīṃ dinātyaye
translation: At the end of (Brahmā’s) day, assuming a bit of the tamo-guṇa, he restrains his prowess, and with everything else withdrawn in him due to the force of kāla, he keeps quiet.

devanagari: तमेवान्वपि धीयन्ते लोका भूरादयस्त्रयः । निशायां अनुवृत्तायां निर्मुक्तशशिभास्करम् ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: tamevānvapi dhīyante lokā bhūrādayastrayaḥniśāyāṃ anuvṛttāyāṃ nirmuktaśaśibhāskaram

devanagari: त्रिलोक्यां दह्यमानायां शक्त्या सङ्कर्षणाग्निना । यान्त्यूष्मणा महर्लोकात् जनं भृग्वादयोऽर्दिताः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: trilokyāṃ dahyamānāyāṃ śaktyā saṅkarṣaṇāgnināyāntyūṣmaṇā maharlokāt janaṃ bhṛgvādayo'rditāḥ
translation: When the three worlds get consumed by the fire from the mouth of Saṅkarṣaṇa (Śeṣa) who is his power, Bhṛgu and others, being distressed by the heat goto Jana-loka from Mahar- loka.

devanagari: तावत् त्रिभुवनं सद्यः कल्पान्तैधितसिन्धवः । प्लावयन्त्युत्कटाटोप चण्डवातेरितोर्मयः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: tāvat tribhuvanaṃ sadyaḥ kalpāntaidhitasindhavaḥplāvayantyutkaṭāṭopa caṇḍavāteritormayaḥ
translation: In the meanwhile, due to Pralaya (world-end), oceans, with huge billows whipped up by extremely terrible and powerful, winds, overflow and inundate the three worlds.

devanagari: अन्तः स तस्मिन् सलिल आस्तेऽनन्तासनो हरिः । योगनिद्रानिमीलाक्षः स्तूयमानो जनालयैः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: antaḥ sa tasmin salila āste'nantāsano hariḥyoganidrānimīlākṣaḥ stūyamāno janālayaiḥ
translation: On that water, on the bed of the serpent Śeṣa, lies Hari with his eyes closed in yogic sleep, while sages in the Janaloka sing his praise.

devanagari: एवंविधैरहोरात्रैः कालगत्योपलक्षितैः । अपक्षितमिवास्यापि परमायुर्वयःशतम् ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃvidhairahorātraiḥ kālagatyopalakṣitaiḥapakṣitamivāsyāpi paramāyurvayaḥśatam
translation: In due course of time, with such types of days and nights as described above, even the long span of life of hundred years of this (god Brahmā), comes to an end.

devanagari: यदर्धमायुषस्तस्य परार्धमभिधीयते । पूर्वः परार्धोऽपक्रान्तो ह्यपरोऽद्य प्रवर्तते ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: yadardhamāyuṣastasya parārdhamabhidhīyatepūrvaḥ parārdho'pakrānto hyaparo'dya pravartate
translation: Half of the life (of god Brahmā) is called parārdha. The first parārdha (of his life) has passed. Now the other half is running.

devanagari: पूर्वस्यादौ परार्धस्य ब्राह्मो नाम महानभूत् । कल्पो यत्राभवद्‍ब्रह्मा शब्दब्रह्मेति यं विदुः ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: pūrvasyādau parārdhasya brāhmo nāma mahānabhūtkalpo yatrābhavad‍brahmā śabdabrahmeti yaṃ viduḥ
translation: At the beginning of the previous parārdha, there was the great kalpa called Brahma kalpa, as Brahmā was then born. They knew him as Śabda-Brahma.

devanagari: तस्यैव चान्ते कल्पोऽभूद् यं पाद्ममभिचक्षते । यद्धरेर्नाभिसरस आसीत् लोकसरोरुहम् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: tasyaiva cānte kalpo'bhūd yaṃ pādmamabhicakṣateyaddharernābhisarasa āsīt lokasaroruham
translation: At the end of that kalpa, there was another kalpa called Padma kalpa, as there sprouted up the world-lotus from the lake-like navel of Hari.

devanagari: अयं तु कथितः कल्पो द्वितीयस्यापि भारत । वाराह इति विख्यातो यत्रासीत् शूकरो हरिः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: ayaṃ tu kathitaḥ kalpo dvitīyasyāpi bhāratavārāha iti vikhyāto yatrāsīt śūkaro hariḥ
translation: Oh Vidura, the present kalpa of the second parārdha is known as Varāha (pertaining to the boar), as Hari assumed the boar-form in this (kalpa).

devanagari: कालोऽयं द्विपरार्धाख्यो निमेष उपचर्यते । अव्याकृतस्यानन्तस्य अनादेर्जगदात्मनः ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: kālo'yaṃ dviparārdhākhyo nimeṣa upacaryateavyākṛtasyānantasya anāderjagadātmanaḥ
translation: The period called dvi-parārdha is regarded as (a negligible period like) a wink of the unmanifested, infinite beginningless Soul of the Universe.

devanagari: कालोऽयं परमाण्वादिः द्विपरार्धान्त ईश्वरः । नैवेशितुं प्रभुर्भूम्न ईश्वरो धाममानिनाम् ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: kālo'yaṃ paramāṇvādiḥ dviparārdhānta īśvaraḥnaiveśituṃ prabhurbhūmna īśvaro dhāmamāninām
translation: This kāla beginning from paramāṇu (its lowest unit) upto the end dvi-parārdha (duration of Brahmadeva’s life) can control those who have attachment to house etc. He has no power over the Almighty God, the bhūman.

devanagari: विकारैः सहितो युक्तैः विशेषादिभिरावृतः । आण्डकोशो बहिरयं पञ्चाशत्कोटिविस्तृतः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: vikāraiḥ sahito yuktaiḥ viśeṣādibhirāvṛtaḥāṇḍakośo bahirayaṃ pañcāśatkoṭivistṛtaḥ
translation: This egg of the universe consisting of sixteen vikāras[8] (modifications) and eight prakṛtis[9] which is covered from outside by the (seven) sheaths of the earth etc., is fifty crores (of yojanas) in breadth.
purport:
Footnote [8]:
viz., mind, ten organs of knowledge and action and five elements (mahābhūtas).

Footnote [9]:
viz., Prakṛti, mahat, ahaṅkāra and five characteristics of elements (e.g. sound, touch etc.).

devanagari: दशोत्तराधिकैर्यत्र प्रविष्टः परमाणुवत् । लक्ष्यतेऽन्तर्गताश्चान्ये कोटिशो ह्यण्डराशयः ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: daśottarādhikairyatra praviṣṭaḥ paramāṇuvatlakṣyate'ntargatāścānye koṭiśo hyaṇḍarāśayaḥ
translation: Each of these sheaths (covering the universe) is ten times greater (than its previous one). This universe appears as an atom when merged (in him). Crores of such universes lie in him. That is the highest essential form of Viṣṇu, the great Puruṣa. It is called the imperishable Brahman, the cause of all causes.

devanagari: तदाहुरक्षरं ब्रह्म सर्वकारणकारणम् । विष्णोर्धाम परं साक्षात् पुरुषस्य महात्मनः ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: tadāhurakṣaraṃ brahma sarvakāraṇakāraṇamviṣṇordhāma paraṃ sākṣāt puruṣasya mahātmanaḥ
translation:
Each of these sheaths (covering the universe) is ten times greater (than its previous one). This universe appears as an atom when merged (in him). Crores of such universes lie in him. That is the highest essential form of Viṣṇu, the great Puruṣa. It is called the imperishable Brahman, the cause of all causes.
[back to top]
